perturbation.original_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end five (5) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end one (1) year after the date of first disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Singapore law, which does not specify a duration but requires reasonable protection, generally understood to be more than one year for certain information.
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore High Court decision in Clearlab S.S. Pte Ltd v Ting Chong Chai [2015] SGHC 195
perturbation.law_explanation: The Clearlab case underscores that the duration of protection for trade secrets must be reasonable considering the nature of the information and the efforts to maintain secrecy. Reducing the protection period to one year could be deemed unreasonable, especially for complex or valuable trade secrets, potentially undermining their legal protection under Singapore law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a five-year duration for obligations, while the changed text reduces it to one year. The Law Explanation, referencing the Clearlab case, states that the duration of protection for trade secrets must be reasonable and reducing it to one year could be deemed unreasonable. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this duration may be insufficient under Singapore law, creating an inconsistency in the contract's terms. Since the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, and the reduced duration is considered unreasonable, there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Singapore . All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by 3 (three) arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The seat, or legal pla ce, of arbitration shall be Singapore , Singapore . The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Singapore . All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled by litigation in a court of the Discloser's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the arbitration clause and stating that disputes will be settled by litigation in a court of the Discloser's choosing may be unenforceable if it is deemed unconscionable or violates principles of fairness and equality under Singapore law.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principles in Singapore as applied to contractual fairness
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law does not have a codified law on unconscionability, the common law principles address unfair contractual terms. A clause allowing the Discloser to unilaterally choose the court and litigation as the dispute resolution method may be challenged if it creates a significant imbalance of power and prejudice against the Recipient, potentially rendering it unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court , which articulated a three-part test for determining whether California workers were independent contractors or employees for purposes of California labor law. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from arbitration to litigation, at the Discloser's choosing, contradicts fairness principles under Singapore common law. The Law Explanation states this choice 'may be challenged if it creates a significant imbalance of power and prejudice against the Recipient, potentially rendering it unenforceable'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to common law as 'law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes'. The original contract provided for a neutral arbitration process, while the changed text gives the Discloser unilateral control over dispute resolution, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return or Destruction. Upon Disc loser’s written request, a Recipient shall promptly return all Confidential Information received under this Agreement, together with all copies, or certify in writing that it has destroyed all such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return or Destruction. Upon Discloser’s written request, a Recipient may retain all Confidential Information received under this Agreement indefinitely, regardless of its destruction, unless compelled by a valid Singapore court order.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing the Recipient to indefinitely retain confidential information, even after a written request for its return or destruction, contradicts the principles of good faith and reasonable protection of confidential information under Singapore law, and increases the risk of unintended breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore law on Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principles in Singapore as applied to Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore law recognizes the tort of breach of confidence. Retaining confidential information against the Discloser's express request can be seen as a breach of the duty of confidence, even if there is no active disclosure. This is because it increases the risk of misuse or accidental disclosure, particularly after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request. The original text mandates the recipient to return or destroy, while the changed text allows indefinite retention unless a Singapore court order compels otherwise. The Law Explanation states that retaining confidential information against the Discloser's express request can be seen as a breach of the duty of confidence. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions common law derived from judicial decisions. While it doesn't directly address the issue of confidential information, it establishes that legal obligations can arise from court decisions, and the 'breach of confidence' falls within the scope of common law. Therefore, the change weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes no obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Informat ion that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is rightfully received by the Recipient from a third party that has no applicable duty of confidentiality; (c) is or becomes the subject of a patent, in which case a party shall rely upon its rights under applicable patent law; (d) is disclosed by the Recipient with the prior written consent of the Discloser. A party also may disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other co mpulsory legal process, provided that if legally permitted to do so it first notify the Discloser in writing at least ten (10) days in advance in order to afford Discloser an opportunity to seek a protective order or other relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes no obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Information. A party also may disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other compulsory legal process without notifying the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notification requirement before disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion contradicts principles of good faith and potentially relevant laws concerning protection of confidential information in legal proceedings in Singapore.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied duty of good faith in Singapore contract law
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Case Law on Implied Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law doesn't have a generalized doctrine of good faith, there is an implied duty to act in good faith in contractual performance. Removing the notification requirement prevents the Discloser from taking steps to protect its confidential information in legal proceedings, potentially violating this implied duty of good faith by denying them a chance to apply for protective orders. Also, some statutes may require notification to the party whose information is being disclosed.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the notification requirement before disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion. The Law Explanation states this may violate the implied duty of good faith and denies the Discloser a chance to apply for protective orders. The Perturbation Explanation suggests the removal contradicts principles of good faith and relevant laws concerning protection of confidential information in legal proceedings in Singapore. While the scraped snippets don't directly confirm a specific law requiring notification, the absence of such laws doesn't negate the potential breach of implied good faith or the deprivation of a right to seek protection, which are legal principles at play. Thus, the removal of the notification creates a contradiction.
